title: The Aldgate Pavilion
summary: A civic pavilion designed to activate a neglected urban square through a folded-plate roof structure that channels rainwater into a central reflecting pool.
challenge: The brief called for a building that could serve multiple civic functions across the day — market hall at noon, performance space at dusk — without the visual weight of traditional civic architecture.
solution: A folded-plate steel roof structure was developed that cantilevers 14m over a permeable granite floor. The roof geometry channels rainwater via concealed downpipes into a central reflecting pool, creating a focal point that changes with light and season.
outcome: The pavilion has transformed footfall in the square by 340% since opening, won a 2024 Sustainable Architecture Commendation, and is now used by over 200 community groups annually.

title: Harrow House Restoration
summary: Sensitive restoration and contemporary extension of a Grade II listed Georgian farmhouse. New volumes read as honest insertions — dark-stained oak and rammed earth against original brick and flint.
challenge: The Georgian farmhouse required significant structural repair, but the client also needed a substantial contemporary extension for a growing family without compromising the heritage setting or the listed building consent.
solution: Rather than mimicking the original, we designed a single-storey linking pavilion in dark-stained green oak and rammed earth — materials that read as genuinely new additions, respecting the host building without imitating it.
outcome: Listed building consent was granted at first application. The project was shortlisted for the NLA Don't Move, Improve! Award 2023 and has been featured in Grand Designs Magazine.

title: Essex Waterside Studios
summary: A cluster of creative production studios arranged around a shared courtyard on a former industrial waterfront. Passive ventilation and green-planted roofs integrate the complex into the riverbank ecology.
challenge: The client needed flexible production studio space that could attract high-end creative tenants while achieving BREEAM Excellent and remaining commercially viable on a contaminated brownfield site.
solution: Five pavilions of varying scale are arranged around a central courtyard. Passive ventilation stacks exploit the river breeze; exposed thermal mass in the concrete frame provides natural temperature regulation; biodiverse roofs extend the riverbank habitat.
outcome: The scheme achieved BREEAM Excellent, a 42% reduction in operational energy vs. baseline, and full pre-let to creative tenants before practical completion.

title: Dubai Cultural Centre
summary: A 12,000 sqm arts complex designed around the idea of the medina — an interior city of shaded passages, courtyards, and intimate performance rooms.
challenge: Dubai's extreme climate and the need for a major public arts institution that feels genuinely rooted in local culture — not an imported Western model — demanded a fundamentally different organisational approach.
solution: We organised the programme around a series of shaded courtyards — the medina model — with perforated aluminium screens filtering light across the day. The screens create shifting geometric patterns, referencing Islamic geometry without literal pastiche.
outcome: Planning approved March 2024. The project is currently on site with practical completion expected Q4 2025.

title: Queen's Park Mews
summary: Four new-build mews houses inserted into the gap between a Victorian terrace and a former stable block. Each house has a distinct internal section and a different relationship to light.
challenge: The narrow infill site — 8m wide and bounded by party walls — required four genuinely individual family homes, each with private outdoor space and adequate natural light, within a constrained budget.
solution: Each house has a different section: a different relationship between stair, gallery, and light well. All four share a communal garden at the rear, secured through a PD amendment that required extensive community consultation.
outcome: All four houses sold off-plan. One home achieved a SAP rating of A92 — the highest in the borough for its typology. The project was featured in the Architects' Journal.
